model_narrative: We run a refinery that turns raw materials into finished products to meet demand that is uncertain and described by a scenario tree. In each time period we decide how much of each raw material to purchase, and for each product at each scenario node we decide how much to obtain by outsourcing when in-house production falls short. Purchasing raw materials incurs a per-unit cost, and outsourcing incurs a per-unit cost that we weight by the probability of each scenario. The objective is to minimize the total expected cost over the whole horizon.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the raw materials available for purchase

sets.name: j
sets.doc: the finished products that must be supplied to meet demand

sets.name: t
sets.doc: the time periods in the planning horizon

sets.name: n
sets.doc: the nodes of the scenario tree, each a possible realization of uncertain demand

sets.name: tn
sets.doc: the valid pairs of time period and scenario-tree node; a node belongs to exactly one period, so this set says which period each node sits in

params.name: c
params.doc: the present purchase cost per unit of each raw material, in dollars per unit

params.name: a
params.doc: the amount of product obtained per unit of raw material, giving for each product and raw material the yield of that product from one unit of that material

params.name: f
params.doc: the cost per unit of meeting product demand by outsourcing in a given period, in dollars per unit

params.name: b
params.doc: the total raw-material inventory capacity, an upper bound on the combined quantity of all raw materials purchased across the whole horizon, in units

params.name: sprob
params.doc: the probability of each scenario-tree node, used to weight outsourcing costs into an expected value

params.name: dem
params.doc: the demand for each product at each scenario-tree node, in units; this is the stochastic quantity that varies across nodes

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the quantity of each raw material purchased for use in each time period

vars.name: y
vars.doc: the quantity of each product obtained by outsourcing, for each valid period-node pair

vars.name: cost
vars.doc: the total expected cost over the horizon, in dollars

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: The combined quantity of all raw materials purchased, added up over every raw material and every time period, must not exceed the available raw-material inventory capacity.

constraints.name: a[jj,
constraints.intent: For each product in each time period and scenario node, the product made in house from the raw materials used in that period together with the amount obtained by outsourcing must be at least the demand for that product at that node. The in-house amount is the total across all raw materials of how much of the product is yielded from the quantity of that material used in that period.

constraints.name: c[i]
constraints.intent: The total expected cost is the cost of all raw-material purchases plus the expected cost of all outsourcing. The purchasing part values every quantity of raw material bought in every period at that material's unit cost. The outsourcing part values every outsourced quantity at its product-and-period unit cost and weights it by the probability of its scenario node. Set the total cost equal to the sum of these two parts.

query: We're considering a spot-market exposure limit — total finished feed bought outside held to 980 units across all scenarios. What's the cost under that ceiling?
expected_pyomo: model.spot_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.y[idx] for idx in model.y) <= 980)

query: We lean on the spot market pretty hard. Couldn't we just hold total outside feed purchases to 1020 units and rely more on our own mixing? What's the tradeoff I'm missing?
expected_pyomo: model.spot_cap1020 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.y[idx] for idx in model.y) <= 1020)
